audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel chapter 17, first-pass restored draft
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for MT/Greek length differences, Goliath height variants, David's age ambiguity, royal reward, sibling bullying, ethnic rhetoric, animal-killing claims, armor and sling, beheading, corpse exposure, pursuit, plunder, Jerusalem chronology, and Saul's identity questions.

verses.restored: Philistines gathered their camps for battle. They gathered at Socoh of Judah and camped between Socoh and Azekah, at Ephes-dammim.
Saul and men of Israel gathered and camped in valley of Elah, arranging battle against Philistines.
Philistines stood on mountain on one side, Israel stood on mountain on other side, and valley was between them.
A champion came out from camps of Philistines, named Goliath of Gath; his height was six cubits and a span.
A bronze helmet was upon his head, and he wore scale armor; weight of armor was five thousand shekels of bronze.
Bronze greaves were upon his legs, and a bronze javelin was slung between his shoulders.
Shaft of his spear was like weaver's beam, iron of spearhead weighed six hundred shekels, and shield-bearer went before him.

He stood and called to Israel's ranks, saying, Why have you come out to arrange battle? Am I not Philistine, and are you not servants of Saul? Choose a man for yourselves, and let him come down to me.
If he is able to fight with me and strike me, we will become your servants; but if I prevail and strike him, you will become our servants and serve us.
Philistine said, I defy ranks of Israel this day. Give me a man, and let us fight together.
Saul and all Israel heard these words of Philistine, and they were shattered and greatly afraid.
David was son of an Ephrathite man from Bethlehem in Judah, whose name was Jesse, who had eight sons. In days of Saul, the man was old, advanced among men.
Three oldest sons of Jesse had gone after Saul to battle. Names of his three sons who went were Eliab firstborn, Abinadab second, and Shammah third.
David was youngest. The three oldest went after Saul.
David went back and forth from Saul to tend flock of his father at Bethlehem.
Philistine drew near morning and evening and presented himself forty days.
Jesse said to David his son, Take for your brothers an ephah of this roasted grain and these ten loaves, and run to camp to your brothers.
Bring these ten portions of cheese to commander of thousand. Visit your brothers for well-being, and take their pledge.
Saul, they, and all men of Israel were in valley of Elah, fighting with Philistines.
David rose early in morning, left flock with keeper, carried supplies, and went as Jesse commanded him. He came to wagon-circle as army was going out to battle line and raising war cry.
Israel and Philistines arranged rank against rank.
David left baggage from upon him in hand of baggage keeper, ran to battle line, and came and asked his brothers about well-being.
As he spoke with them, champion came up—Goliath Philistine from Gath by name—from ranks of Philistines, and spoke according to same words. David heard.
All men of Israel, when they saw the man, fled from before him and were greatly afraid.

Men of Israel said, Have you seen this man who comes up? He comes up to defy Israel. The man who strikes him, king will enrich with great wealth, give him his daughter, and make house of his father free in Israel.

David said to men standing with him, What will be done for man who strikes this Philistine and turns aside disgrace from Israel? For who is this uncircumcised Philistine that he defies ranks of living Divine?
People answered him according to this word, saying, Thus shall it be done for man who strikes him.

Eliab his oldest brother heard when he spoke to men, and Eliab's anger burned against David. He said, Why did you come down, and with whom did you leave those few sheep in wilderness? I know your presumption and evil of your heart, for you came down to see battle.
David said, What have I done now? Was it not only a word?
He turned from beside him toward another and spoke according to same word, and people returned an answer as first word.
Words David spoke were heard and reported before Saul, and he sent for him.
David said to Saul, Let no human heart fall because of him. Your servant will go and fight with this Philistine.
Saul said to David, You are not able to go against this Philistine to fight with him, for you are a youth and he is a man of war from his youth.
David said to Saul, Your servant was tending his father's flock. Lion or bear came and carried a sheep from flock.
I went out after it, struck it, and rescued from its mouth. When it rose against me, I seized its beard, struck it, and put it to death.
Your servant struck both lion and bear. This uncircumcised Philistine shall be like one of them, because he defied ranks of living Divine.
David said, YHWH who rescued me from hand of lion and hand of bear will rescue me from hand of this Philistine. Saul said to David, Go, and may YHWH be with you.
Saul clothed David with his garments, placed a bronze helmet upon his head, and clothed him with armor.

David girded his sword over his garments and tried to walk, because he had not tested them. David said to Saul, I cannot walk in these, because I have not tested them. David removed them from upon him.
He took his staff in his hand, chose five smooth stones from wadi, put them in shepherd's bag he had—in pouch—and his sling was in his hand. He drew near to Philistine.
Philistine came, coming and drawing near to David, and man bearing shield was before him.
Philistine looked and saw David and despised him, because he was a youth, ruddy, and beautiful in appearance.
Philistine said to David, Am I a dog, that you come to me with sticks? Philistine cursed David by his gods.
Philistine said to David, Come to me, and I will give your flesh to birds of heavens and animals of field.
David said to Philistine, You come to me with sword, spear, and javelin, but I come to you in name of YHWH of armies, Divine of ranks of Israel, whom you have defied.

This day YHWH will deliver you into my hand. I will strike you, remove your head from upon you, and give corpses of Philistine camp this day to birds of heavens and animals of earth, so all earth may know there is Divine for Israel.
All this assembly shall know that YHWH does not save by sword or spear, because battle belongs to YHWH, and he will give you into our hand.
When Philistine arose and came and drew near to meet David, David hurried and ran toward battle line to meet Philistine.
David put his hand into bag, took from there a stone, slung it, and struck Philistine on forehead. Stone sank into his forehead, and he fell upon his face to ground.
David prevailed over Philistine with sling and stone, struck Philistine, and put him to death. There was no sword in David's hand.
David ran, stood over Philistine, took his sword, drew it from sheath, put him to death, and cut off his head with it. Philistines saw their champion was dead and fled.
Men of Israel and Judah arose, shouted, and pursued Philistines as far as entrance of valley and gates of Ekron. Wounded Philistines fell along road of Shaaraim as far as Gath and Ekron.
Children of Israel returned from hot pursuit of Philistines and plundered their camp.
David took head of Philistine and brought it to Jerusalem, but he put his weapons in his tent.
When Saul saw David going out to meet Philistine, he said to Abner commander of army, Whose son is this youth, Abner? Abner said, As your life, king, I do not know.
King said, Ask whose son this young man is.
When David returned from striking Philistine, Abner took him and brought him before Saul, with head of Philistine in his hand.
Saul said to him, Whose son are you, youth? David said, Son of your servant Jesse the Bethlehemite.

translation_notes: The chapter exists in substantially shorter form in important Greek witnesses; verses and narrative seams involving David's prior court service, the reward, and Saul's questions require source-witness audit.
Goliath's reported height differs across witnesses. Physical size is narrative military description, not proof of moral worth, monstrosity, disability, or Divine rejection.
David is called a youth, but his precise age is not given. This story must not romanticize child soldiers, recruit minors into danger, or teach that courage means accepting adult warfare.
Eliab's contempt and Goliath's ridicule are bullying and status enforcement. Sibling conflict, age, skin tone, beauty, height, and body size must not be used to shame people.
'Uncircumcised Philistine' is ancient covenant-war rhetoric and may not become an ethnic, religious, or bodily slur against living people.
The challenge, reward, royal daughter, and household exemption show political and economic incentives surrounding David's choice; the scene is not a pure individual duel outside systems of power.
David rejects unfamiliar armor but still uses a lethal ranged weapon. The narrative does not teach weapon invulnerability, magical stones, or that faith makes safety planning unnecessary.
The beheading, corpse exposure, pursuit, casualties, and plunder are battlefield violence. They may not be glorified, imitated, or sanitized into harmless children's entertainment.
Verse 47 critiques trust in military technology, yet the narrative still culminates in killing. It cannot authorize holy war, vigilantism, ethnic violence, or leader cults.
Saul's questions about David's father likely concern household identity and reward obligations; they also participate in source tensions with chapter 16.
safety_note: Do not use David and Goliath to bully bodies, shame fear, recruit children, glorify weapons, promise supernatural invulnerability, or label a modern group as Philistines. David's courage does not erase the political rewards, battlefield deaths, beheading, corpse exposure, pursuit, or plunder. The story cannot justify holy war, vigilantism, racial or religious contempt, or the claim that a favored young hero is beyond accountability.
